title: Simran Chhabra Murder Case
description:
Simran was killed by Rohit Lalit alias Langda. This was our first major success in the criminal trial of cases of Medical Negligence. Their family met us on 27th August 2023, exactly after 8 months of her unfortunate and untimely death. Despite killing Simran, the accused was intimidating her family. He had filed false criminal cases against them. With grave difficulty the family was able to get their complaint registered before Medical Negligence Board. But the problems with these boards are, either they DO NOT want to make inquiries against their fraternity or they are made to shut their mouth because of the bribes they are paid. In this case also a Medical Negligence Inquiry report was  made which was completely sham.When we took up the matter, our main advocate, Nishant Bharihoke was brutally assaulted and the accused thorough his goons even tried to kill him. With a negative report in hand, FIR against one of the relative and constant threat to the life of anyone who came forward made the case all the more difficult. Though bare looking at the report even a dumbest person would have said that the report was bogus, still the courts seemingly bound by the Jacob Mathews judgement, refused to entertain our criminal complaint as the report exonerated the doctor. We prayed to the court to setup another medical board. The Court ordered PGI, Chandigarh, a premier healthcare institute to inquire, but the PGI Chandigarh even disregarded the orders and put the inquiry into cold. We desperately needed some evidence to break the report.

Since we knew that argumentatively we can break the report, we decided to file a criminal case against the member of the medical board. With the arguments we were able to convince the court to order an inquiry against them u/s 202 of the CrPC. Fortunately for us, the court ordered the inquiry and also deputed ACP, Crime as the inquiry officer.

The ACP, Crime did a fantastic job and brought on record many evidences which convincingly refuted the report. Then we met a doctor and showed him all the evideces and asked only one question, with the documents that we showed, can the findings of the report be corroborated or contradicted. We assured him that we will never ask him to enter the witness box. With that assurance he gave it in writing that findings of the police and the report are contradictory. Since the police had given all un refutable evidences, the court was bound to accept the evidence and the opinion of the doctor as a result, the court issued the summons u/s 304A of the IPC. 

Unfortunately, the accused went to the High Court and the judge sitting there mechanically stayed the trial, this is unfortunate reality of our justice system. As we write this post, its been a year and 6 months the trial is suspended.

The very reason of starting this foundation is to make sufferers holler so hard that the deaf judiciary listens to their plight.

But we are sure that Simran will get the justice. This accused Rohit Lalit alias Langda will definitely go to jail. Not only for medical negligence but under more strict offences  like cheating, forgery, destruction of evidence etc, its just a matter of time.

title: A 10 years old fight of a brother
description:
Meet Mahesh Sharma, a brother who has been relentlessly fighting for justice for the untimely death of his younger brother, Dharmendra Sharma (37).

Dharmendra was a healthy young man who neither smoked nor consumed alcohol. In 2012, he experienced mild abdominal pain, which was diagnosed as small calcifications in the kidney. He was examined by Sarvar Iqbal at Columbia Asia Hospital, Gurgaon.

Sarvar Iqbal treated him for some time and thereafter advised surgery. However, the surgery was delayed because the doctor was shifting his practice to Max Hospital, Gurgaon. On the fateful day of 4 April 2013, Dharmendra was admitted to Max Hospital. He was operated upon on 5 April 2013 at around 12:40 PM. His wife and their young child never imagined that the operation theatre would turn into a living hell for them.

The PCNL surgery (a keyhole procedure), which was expected to last barely one to two hours, continued for several hours. The family was informed that the surgery was over and that Dharmendra would soon be shifted to the recovery room. However, the wait continued until 7:45 PM.

When he was finally brought out, his urine bag was full of blood. The family panicked and questioned the nursing staff, who insensitively responded that it was “normal.” Dharmendra writhed in pain throughout the night and repeatedly called Sarvar Iqbal for help. However, the doctor appeared more concerned with his duty in his bedroom than his duty of care towards the patient. Dharmendra was administered heavy painkillers and opioids and was put to sleep.

By the next morning, Dharmendra was nearly dead. The ICU team arrived at around 9:30 AM, but there was very little left to save. The hospital kept him alive until 20 June 2013—the day he was finally relieved of his pain and suffering. By then, he had lost a kidney, his intestine, and was suffering from severe infection and cerebral oedema.

After a prolonged struggle, Mahesh Sharma succeeded in getting an FIR registered. However, without a report from a medical board, no effective action could be taken. Fortunately, in 2014, the medical board submitted its report, which—though cryptic—did hold Sarvar Iqbal negligent. Unfortunately, Mahesh’s ill-informed legal counsel did not wait for the process to mature and advised him to escalate the matter to the MCI.

The MCI— the Most Corrupt Institution in the country—acting in complete disregard of ethics and morality, exonerated Sarvar Iqbal.

The case dragged on for ten long years, with Mahesh enduring the harsh realities of the criminal justice system—a quintessentially victim's victimisation.

We faced a major hurdle. Unless the false MCI report was demolished, there was absolutely no chance of justice for Mahesh. Being a State case, the prosecution was led by the Public Prosecutor. With due respect, given their overwhelming workload, it is unreasonable to expect them to master and argue complex medical terminology in a criminal courtroom.

In 2024, we took over the criminal trial, by which time considerable damage had already been done. It was an extremely complex case. In the final phase, we played our hidden card. 

Sarvar Iqbal believed he was sailing smoothly through the trial and, to ensure that no opportunity arose for cross-examination, he chose not to lead any defence evidence. Strategically, it was a clever move—no evidence meant no cross-examination and no scope for error.

We were completely at a loss. Then came an epiphany. We decided to summon the MCI consultant, Dr. Hans Raj Baweja, as a prosecution witness.

The judiciary, at any level—from the Magistrate to the Apex Court—can be defined in any way but glory. But, in this case, we were fortunate that the presiding Magistrate was one of those few judges who command genuine respect. He permitted us to examine Dr. Baweja. The witness was too fragile to withstand rigorous scrutiny, and through examination, we were able to conclusively establish that the MCI report was nothing but trash.

As of today, final arguments have concluded. Written submissions along with all relevant case laws have been filed. We now await the judgment.

If we succeed, this may well become one of the rare convictions for medical negligence. If we do not, the fight will continue until the accused is taken to the place he deserves—Bhondsi Jail.
